skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automate Twitter/X with posting, engagement, and user management via inference.sh CLI. Apps: x/post-tweet, x/post-create (with media), x/post-like, x/post-retweet, x/dm-send, x/user-follow. Capabilities: post tweets, schedule content, like posts, retweet, send DMs, follow users, get profiles. Use for: social media automation, content scheduling, engagement bots, audience growth, X API. Triggers: twitter api, x api, tweet automation, post to twitter, twitter bot, social media automation, x automation, tweet scheduler, twitter integration, post tweet, twitter post, x post, send tweet
skill.summary: Automate Twitter/X posts, engagement, and user management via belt CLI
skill.author: skills-shell

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 46 potential issues, but all are false positives. The 2 HIGH findings (weak cryptographic algorithm) are YAML frontmatter delimiters and markdown table syntax misinterpreted as code. The 37 MEDIUM findings (Ruby/shell backtick execution) are bash command examples inside markdown code blocks - not actual executions. The 7 LOW findings (hardcoded URLs) are legitimate documentation links to inference.sh and GitHub. No malicious intent detected. The external_commands and network risk factors are inherent to the skill's purpose (Twitter automation via belt CLI), not vulnerabilities.

high_findings.title: YAML Frontmatter Delimiters Misidentified as Cryptographic Code
high_findings.description: Lines 3 (SKILL.md:3) contain YAML frontmatter delimiters (---) which the static scanner misidentified as 'weak cryptographic algorithm' markers. This is a false positive - no cryptographic code exists in this file.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The YAML frontmatter delimiters (---) at the start of the file are standard markdown metadata, not cryptographic code. The static analyzer incorrectly flagged these as crypto-related.

high_findings.title: Markdown Table Syntax Misidentified as Cryptographic Code
high_findings.description: Line 29 (SKILL.md:29) contains markdown table syntax (|-----|--------|-------------|) which the static scanner misidentified as 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. This is a false positive - it is standard markdown formatting.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown table formatting with pipe characters is legitimate documentation syntax, not cryptographic code. The static analyzer incorrectly pattern-matched table borders as crypto indicators.

medium_findings.title: Bash Code Examples Misidentified as Shell Execution
medium_findings.description: 37 detections of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at various lines in SKILL.md. These are bash command examples inside markdown code blocks (```bash ... ```), not actual command executions. The backticks are markdown fences, not shell backticks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines are bash examples displayed as documentation in markdown code blocks. No actual command execution occurs - the belt CLI commands shown are demonstration examples, not executable code within this file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs for Documentation Links
low_findings.description: 7 detections of 'Hardcoded URL' in SKILL.md pointing to inference.sh and GitHub. These are legitimate documentation links to the belt CLI installation page and API documentation. Not a security concern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs point to the official inference.sh documentation and installation guides. These are expected hardcoded links in a documentation file, not security issues.

content.user_title: Automate Twitter/X Posts, Engagement, and User Management
content.value_statement: Managing social media manually takes hours each day. This skill automates Twitter/X posting, likes, retweets, DMs, and follows through the belt CLI so you can focus on creating content.
actual_capabilities: Post text tweets and tweets with media attachments to Twitter/X
Like, retweet, and delete posts programmatically
Send direct messages to Twitter users
Follow and unfollow users based on username
Get tweet details and user profile information by ID or username
Chain with AI image and video generation skills for automated content creation
limitations: Requires belt CLI installation and Twitter/X API credentials configuration
Rate limits from Twitter API apply to all operations
Media URLs must be publicly accessible web URLs - local files not supported

use_cases.title: Automate Content Publishing
use_cases.description: Schedule and auto-post content to Twitter/X without manual login. Generate images with AI, then post them directly to your feed.
use_cases.target_user: Social media managers and content creators

use_cases.title: Build Engagement Automation
use_cases.description: Auto-like, retweet, and follow accounts based on keywords or lists. Respond to mentions automatically with personalized DMs.

use_cases.title: Create Cross-Platform Workflows
use_cases.description: Generate AI images or videos with other skills, then immediately post them to Twitter. Chain multiple skills for complete pipelines.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Simple Tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the twitter-automation skill to post a tweet that says: {tweet_text}

prompt_templates.title: Post AI-Generated Content
prompt_templates.prompt: First use ai-image-generation to create an image of {description}, then use twitter-automation to post it to Twitter with the caption: {caption}

prompt_templates.title: Engage with Target Accounts
prompt_templates.prompt: Use twitter-automation to like and retweet the latest post from username {username}, then follow them and send a DM saying: {message}

prompt_templates.title: Schedule Multiple Posts
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a sequence of {count} tweets about {topic} with varying hooks, then use twitter-automation to post them at {time} intervals. List them in order first.

output_examples.input: Post a tweet announcing our new product launch
output_examples.output: Successfully posted tweet: 'Excited to announce our new product! 🚀 Check it out at example.com/product [image attached]' Tweet ID: 1234567890

output_examples.input: Like and retweet the latest post from OpenAI
output_examples.output: Retweeted: 'We just released GPT-5 with groundbreaking reasoning capabilities...' - 45.2K likes, 8.1K retweets. Also liked the post. User followed: @OpenAI

output_examples.input: Send a welcome DM to new followers
output_examples.output: Sent welcome DM to @newfollower: 'Thanks for following! Let me know if you have any questions about our products.' Status: Delivered

best_practices: Test commands with sample mode before running live: belt app sample x/post-tweet --save input.json
Verify JSON output with --dry-run or sample mode before posting actual content
Review all auto-generated tweet text before posting to catch errors or unintended content
anti_patterns: Do not spam posts rapidly - respect Twitter rate limits and community guidelines
Do not auto-generate repetitive content without review - tweets must sound natural
Do not skip verification before posting - always check sample output first

faq.question: Do I need Twitter/X API credentials?
faq.answer: Yes, you must configure your Twitter/X API credentials in the belt CLI before using this skill. Run belt login to authenticate.

faq.question: Can I post images with tweets?
faq.answer: Yes, use the x/post-create app with a media_url field pointing to a publicly accessible image URL. Local file uploads are not supported.

faq.question: How do I avoid hitting rate limits?
faq.answer: Add delays between actions and check Twitter API status. The skill does not include built-in rate limit handling - implement your own throttling.

faq.question: Can I manage multiple accounts?
faq.answer: Each belt login session uses one account. Switch accounts by running belt logout and belt login with different credentials.

faq.question: How do I test before posting live?
faq.answer: Use belt app sample to see input formats, or run with --dry-run if supported. Always verify output before posting actual content.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use?
faq.answer: Yes, all commands are documented examples shown as text. The skill only executes belt CLI commands when you explicitly call it with a task.
